hook: A three-segment villa from 1928–29 that smuggled Le Corbusier wholesale onto a Warsaw side street — pilotis, ribbon windows, roof terrace and all.
facts: Designed by Bohdan Lachert and Józef Szanajca and built in 1928–1929 as a single block of three family villas (numbers 9, 11 and 11a).

Lachert's own segment (no. 9) openly references Le Corbusier's Villa La Roche (Paris, 1923–24); the building is cited as one of the first Polish realisations of Le Corbusier's 'five points of architecture'.
Each segment has a two-storey salon as its core, with apartments of roughly 150 m² over three levels on a reinforced-concrete skeleton.
Built partly of celolit blocks; entered into the heritage register in 1973.
Saska Kępa became the city's testing ground for functionalist housing in the 1930s, and this villa is its textbook example.
w: A three-segment villa of 1928-29 that smuggled Le Corbusier wholesale onto a Saska Kepa side street -- pilotis, ribbon windows, roof terraces, the lot. Lachert and Szanajca built it as three linked family houses; Lachert's own wing openly quotes the Villa La Roche. One of the first Polish buildings to obey Corbusier's 'five points'.

hook: A neo-Renaissance tsarist veterinary college on Grochowska, abandoned for years, now the home of one of Poland's best orchestras and the half-built shell of its biggest concert hall.
facts: The complex was built in 1898–1900 to a design by Vladimir Pokrowski in historicising neo-Renaissance forms on the Grochów/Kamionek border.
It housed the tsarist Warsaw Veterinary Institute, then successive university veterinary faculties — latterly the SGGW faculty, which left in 2005.
In March 2009 the City of Warsaw bought the derelict buildings from SGGW for 15 million złoty and handed them to the Sinfonia Varsovia orchestra, its seat since 2010.
A 2010 international competition for a 1,800+ seat concert hall, judged from 138 entries, was won by Atelier Thomas Pucher of Graz; construction began in 2023.
Located between Grochowska and Terespolska streets, beside Park Obwodu Praga AK.
w: A neo-Renaissance tsarist veterinary college on Grochowska, derelict for years, now home to one of Poland's finest orchestras -- and the half-built shell of its biggest concert hall. Built 1898-1900 for the Tsar's vets, abandoned in 2005, bought by the city in 2009. Its 1,800-seat hall has been rising in the yard since 2023.

hook: Jan Wedel's 1931 super-modern chocolate works above Jeziorko Kamionkowskie — the building where Ptasie Mleczko was invented and where the Wedel anchor still presides.
facts: A super-modern confectionery plant designed by engineer Józef Napoleon Czerwiński and built 1927–31 for E. Wedel; Jan Wedel moved the firm's address to Zamoyskiego in 1931.
The Wedel chocolate brand traces to 1851, when Karol Wedel opened his first Warsaw manufactory.
Jan Wedel devised the recipe for Ptasie Mleczko ('bird's milk') marshmallow inside this factory in 1936.
The site has been owned since 2010 by the Lotte group and still operates as a working factory; a Wedel chocolate museum opened on the premises in September 2024.
The plant stands just east of Jeziorko Kamionkowskie, with its public entrance from ul. Lubelska.
w: Jan Wedel's super-modern chocolate works of 1931, rising above Jeziorko Kamionkowskie, the firm's anchor over the door. The Wedel name goes back to an 1851 Warsaw manufactory; inside this very building, in 1936, Jan invented Ptasie Mleczko, the 'bird's milk' marshmallow. Still a working factory, with a chocolate museum since 2024.

hook: A life-size stone Madonna funded in 1908 by factory workers to stop their workplace from collapsing — Praga's most storied street shrine, shoehorned into a clipped building corner.
facts: A life-size stone figure of the Mother of God with Child, carved in 1908 by sculptor Feliks Giecewicz, paid for with modest contributions from workers of the 'Labor' factory.
It stands at the meeting of four streets — Radzymińska, Kawęczyńska, Korsaka and Ząbkowska — in Szmulowizna.
The pedestal bears the inscription 'O MATKO WYJEDNAJ NAM PRZEBACZENIE WIN POKÓJ I MIŁOŚĆ PANA JEZUSA 1908'.
Until 1934 it stood on the opposite side of Ząbkowska; it was moved to its present clipped-corner spot because it blocked the pavement.
It is one of the best-known of over 100 surviving devotional shrines (kapliczki) of Praga.
legend: Local tradition holds the figure replaced an older wooden cross said to mark either victims of the 1794 Praga massacre or the spot where Tadeusz Korsak fell on 4 November 1794; the statue was reputedly raised to keep the factory from collapsing.
w: A life-size stone Madonna that Praga factory workers paid for in 1908, shoehorned into a clipped corner where four roads meet. The story goes she replaced an older cross marking victims of the 1794 massacre -- and that the workers raised her to keep their factory from collapsing. One of over a hundred shrines that still guard Praga.

hook: The moated concrete pit where Warsaw's brown bears paced beside a six-lane avenue for almost 70 years — emptied in 2020, then painted gold and renamed the Golden Island.
facts: The enclosure was built by the firm 'Betonstal' and completed in May 1952 as a roadside attraction for the newly opened W-Z route, measuring about 50 by 15 metres.
A free-to-view concrete platform ringed by a deep water moat, it acted as a calling card luring passers-by on Al. Solidarności toward the Warsaw Zoo.
The last bears, Sabina and Mała — circus animals of around 37 — were moved into the zoo proper in 2020–21 after an unauthorised intrusion, leaving the pit empty.
In August 2023 artist Paweł Althamer, with Zachęta gallery and the zoo, had volunteers paint the abandoned enclosure gold as 'Złota Wyspa' (Golden Island), a temporary space for quiet.
The bears were such a fixture that a nearby block carries a mural titled 'Widzi MiSię Praga'.
w: For almost seventy years brown bears paced this moated concrete pit beside a six-lane avenue, a 1952 roadside lure pulling passers-by toward the zoo. The last two went inside in 2020. Then in 2023 Pawel Althamer had volunteers paint the empty enclosure gold and rename it the Golden Island -- a place for quiet, where bears once paced.

hook: A 1906 corner tenement built for a prince and crowned with winged owls, its façade crawling with bats and dragons — Praga eclecticism at full theatrical pitch.
facts: An ornate corner tenement erected around 1906 for Prince Bronisław Massalski as a rental building, at the corner of today's Okrzei and Jagiellońska (formerly Brukowa and Petersburska).
It takes its name from the spread-winged owl sculptures crowning the façade; the elevations also carry bas-reliefs of bats and dragons and a domed corner bay.
Between the wars its ground floor held the restaurant 'U Abrama', run by a man named Kronenberg.
After WWII it became communal flats and stood derelict for years before a Fenix Group restoration returned the owls and added a floor, yielding 42 apartments.
Its decoration blends Art Nouveau (secesja) with eclecticism.
w: A 1906 corner tenement built for a prince and crowned with spread-winged stone owls, its facade crawling with bats and dragons. Between the wars its ground floor held a restaurant; after it, decades as communal flats and dereliction. A recent restoration brought the owls back and added a floor. Secesja with fangs.

hook: A 59-hectare alder-and-birch wood that is also a battlefield — the marshy ground where some 15,000 men fell on a single February day in 1831.
facts: A 59.4-hectare landscape reserve established by ministerial decree of 24 November 1983, effective 1 January 1984.
It was created to protect, for historical and landscape reasons, fragments of woodland on the battlefield of the all-day engagement of 25 February 1831 during the November Uprising.
Roughly 15,000 Polish and Russian soldiers were killed or wounded that day out of about 100,000 engaged.
The reserve lies in a depression on the Vistula's right bank, a former floodplain, with rare European elm (wiąz szypułkowy) among diverse trees and around 40 breeding bird species.
The 1831 battle monument stands at the reserve's southern edge, and Kościuszko-Insurrection earthworks survive on its southern boundary.
w: A 59-hectare alder-and-birch wood on the Vistula's marshy right bank -- and a battlefield. Here, on a single February day in 1831, some 15,000 men fell in the bloodiest engagement of the November Uprising. The reserve was set aside in 1984 to protect both the trees and the ground; the battle monument stands at its southern edge.

hook: An early-modernist cooperative bank crowned with stone eagles that survived the Uprising thanks to its steel-and-concrete frame.
facts: Built 1912-1917 to a design by architect Jan Heurich (the younger) for the Bank Towarzystw Spółdzielczych (Bank of Cooperative Societies).
The corner eagles that give the building its name, and the bas-reliefs of farm labour, were sculpted by Józef Zygmunt Otto.
Around 1923 the facade was clad in copper sheet (by the Żeleziński firm); the decorative metal plates were removed during the 1948-1950 reconstruction.
Its steel reinforced-concrete frame let it survive bombardment during the 1944 Warsaw Uprising.
Rebuilt 1948-1950 and extended toward Sienkiewicza Street to a design by Barbara Brukalska, on the site of the prewar Teatr Mały (Modern Theatre).
Today it houses the Krajowa Rada Spółdzielcza (National Cooperative Council).
w: An early-modernist cooperative bank of 1912-17, crowned with stone eagles and reliefs of farm labour. Its steel-and-concrete frame carried it through the 1944 bombardment when softer buildings fell. The copper sheathing came off in the postwar rebuild, when Barbara Brukalska extended it. Eagles over a bank that outlasted the war.

a: Śródmieście / Mariensztat (al. Solidarności pod placem Zamkowym)
hook: Poland's first escalators, a Soviet gift hauling crowds 12 metres up the Vistula scarp through a socrealist tile tunnel.
facts: Opened 22 July 1949 with the rest of the Trasa W-Z (East-West Route); President Bolesław Bierut presided over the ceremony.
These were the first escalators in Warsaw and Poland, built by the Soviet firm Mietrostroj of Moscow as a gift; the driving machinery weighed about 150 tonnes.
Three runs, each roughly 30 m long, climb a 12 m height difference with a rated capacity of 10,000 people per hour.

The pedestrian tunnel was lined with glazed ceramic tiles and clinker brick and held two gypsum reliefs, 'Razem w walce' (Together in Battle) and 'Razem w odbudowie' (Together in Reconstruction), from Jerzy Jarnuszkiewicz's studio.
The escalators occupied a floor of the John tenement (kamienica Johna, Krakowskie Przedmieście 89), which had to be widened by about 80 cm to fit them.
Closed in 1997, the original Soviet mechanism was replaced with ThyssenKrupp equipment when they reopened in 2005; salvaged parts went to the National Museum of Technology.
w: Poland's first escalators -- a 1949 Soviet gift, 150 tonnes of Moscow machinery hauling crowds up the Vistula scarp through a tunnel lined in glazed tile and socrealist reliefs. They opened with the whole East-West Route, Bierut presiding. The host tenement was widened to fit them. Replaced in 2005, the old gears went to a museum.

hook: Warsaw's grandest stage-set of Stalinist socrealism, where worker reliefs and three giant candelabra stand in for a monument that was never built.
facts: The square and the surrounding Marszałkowska Residential District (MDM) opened on 22 July 1952, the day the PRL constitution was adopted; it was named on 19 July 1952.
Building ground floors carry socialist-realist reliefs and sculptures of idealised workers representing the 'leading working class'.
Three monumental candelabra-shaped lamps stand by the Hotel MDM corner of Marszałkowska and Waryńskiego; Bierut reportedly proposed them after candelabra he had seen in Odessa.
Earlier schemes for the square's centre included a Stalin monument and then three statues representing Śląsk, Pomorze and Stolica, none of which was built.
Hotel MDM was the first hotel raised in postwar reconstructed Warsaw.
Five streets converge on the square: Marszałkowska, Koszykowa, Piękna, Śniadeckich and Waryńskiego.
w: Warsaw's grandest stage-set of Stalinist socrealism, opened on constitution day 1952 -- worker reliefs along the ground floors and three giant candelabra Bierut is said to have copied from Odessa. The square's centre was meant for a Stalin monument, then three allegorical statues; neither came. Five streets pour into it.

hook: The architect-couple's own house, often called Poland's first fully avant-garde building, a De Stijl composition years ahead of its street.
facts: Built 1927-1929 on Żoliborz as the private home of avant-garde architects Barbara and Stanisław Brukalski, members of the Praesens group; structural engineering by Stanisław Hempel.
It is described in the literature as the first fully avant-garde modernist realisation in Poland and one of the most important houses in Polish architectural history.
Its interpenetrating geometric volumes and large glazing were directly inspired by De Stijl, especially Gerrit Rietveld's Rietveld Schröder House in Utrecht.
Stanisław Brukalski also designed several WSM Żoliborz cooperative housing colonies (nos. IV, VII, IX); Barbara Brukalska later led the postwar reconstruction of buildings such as Dom pod Orłami.
The house still stands and remains a landmark of interwar Polish modernism roughly a century after completion.
w: The house the Brukalskis built for themselves in 1927-29, often called Poland's first fully avant-garde building -- a composition of sliding geometric volumes lifted straight from De Stijl and Rietveld's Utrecht house. The architect couple were Praesens-group radicals; Stanislaw went on to design Zoliborz's modernist estates.

hook: A 42-metre brick water tower with a machicolated crown that doubled as a chimney for the steam engines pumping Warsaw's first clean water.
facts: Erected 1885-1886 as part of William Lindley's waterworks; the Lindley system began supplying Warsaw on 3 July 1886.
The tower stands about 42 m tall on a circular plan: a granite base, a red-brick shaft, and a brick head separated by a machicolation-style cornice.
Besides maintaining water pressure in the mains it also served as a chimney venting exhaust from the steam engines that pumped the water.
It used an innovative scheme of connected vertical pipes rather than a top reservoir; the tower went out of use around 1924-1934.
The waterworks were commissioned on the initiative of Warsaw's president Sokrates Starynkiewicz, with construction supervised by Lindley's son William Heerlein Lindley.
The whole Stacja Filtrów complex was recognised as a Pomnik Historii (Monument of History) by presidential decree in 2012; the tower was later restored.
w: A 42-metre brick tower with a machicolated crown, raised in 1886 to hold pressure in Warsaw's first clean-water mains -- and doubling as the chimney venting the steam engines that pumped it. Lindley's waterworks began supplying the city that July, on Starynkiewicz's push. The whole filter complex became a Monument of History in 2012.

hook: The lone surviving entrance pavilion of the largest printing plant in communist Poland, where Trybuna Ludu rolled off the presses.
facts: Dom Słowa Polskiego, the largest polygraphic plant in the PRL, opened on 22 July 1950 on the former plac Kazimierza Wielkiego between Towarowa, Miedziana, Srebrna and Prosta streets.
Designed by Kazimierz Marczewski, Stefan Putowski and Zygmunt Skibniewski; titles printed here included Trybuna Ludu, Sztandar Młodych, Świerszczyk and Płomyczek.
It was modernised and expanded in 1973-1977 and operated until about 2010.
The complex was demolished in 2022-2023, but one entrance pavilion with its characteristic skylights and spiral staircases was kept.
That pavilion is being restored under conservation supervision as the centrepiece of the public Ogrody / Park Słowa Polskiego in the Towarowa 22 development, with public access planned around 2026.
w: The lone surviving entrance pavilion of the largest printing plant in communist Poland, where Trybuna Ludu and a shelf of party titles rolled off the presses from 1950. The vast complex came down in 2022-23; conservators kept this one pavilion, with its skylights and spiral stairs, as the centrepiece of a new public garden.

hook: The older and smaller of Warsaw's two Muslim cemeteries, laid out for the Tsar's Caucasus-born soldiers and still receiving the city's Chechen dead.
facts: Founded 1838-1839 on roughly 0.1 ha of land bought in 1830 from the Evangelical-Augsburg parish, on the initiative of the Russian partition authorities.
Originally created for Muslim soldiers of the Tsarist army garrisoned in Poland; closed to general use around 1867 after about 30 years.
Burials include Jan Buczacki (d. 1851), a Polish Tatar who worked on an early Quran translation, and General Zachariasz Kieński (d. 1857).
It is the older of Warsaw's two Muslim cemeteries (the Tatar cemetery on Tatarska St being the second).
In July 2014 it was recognised, with five neighbouring denominational cemeteries at Powązki, as a Pomnik historii (Monument of History).
Refugees from Chechnya have been buried here in recent decades, keeping the small mizar in active use.
w: The older and smaller of Warsaw's two Muslim cemeteries, laid out in 1838 for the Tsar's Caucasus-born soldiers on a sliver of land bought from the Lutherans. Closed to general use by the 1860s, it holds a Tatar who worked on an early Quran translation. In recent decades the city's Chechen refugees have been buried here.

hook: An open-air contemporary-sculpture park slipped into a 1970s housing-estate park, where a new work appears once a year as another 'chapter'.
facts: Initiated in 2009 by artist Paweł Althamer (a Bródno native) with the Targówek district and the Museum of Modern Art in Warsaw, which manages the collection.
Set inside Park Bródnowski, a green space built 1976-1978 and opened in 1978 in the Targówek housing district.
The project is structured in annual 'chapters' - roughly one new sculpture is added each year; some works are non-permanent or ephemeral.
Contributing artists include Olafur Eliasson, Monika Sosnowska, Rirkrit Tiravanija, Susan Philipsz, Jens Haaning, Katarzyna Przezwańska and the Nowolipie group.
It is open and free to the public 24 hours a day, distinct from (and about 1.8 km from) the historic Bródno Cemetery to the south.
w: An open-air contemporary-sculpture park slipped into a 1970s housing-estate green, where a new work appears once a year as another 'chapter'. The Brodno-born artist Pawel Althamer started it in 2009 with the Museum of Modern Art; Eliasson, Sosnowska and Tiravanija have all added pieces. Free, around the clock, among the tower blocks.

hook: A pentagonal Tsarist fort of the inner Warsaw Fortress ring that later carried Poland's pre-war national radio transmitter on 75-metre masts.
facts: Built in the 1880s as part of the inner ring of Twierdza Warszawa (Warsaw Fortress); a regular pentagon surrounded by a dry moat.
After the fortress modernisation of 1889-1892 it was converted to ammunition storage; partial demolition followed the 1909 decision to liquidate the fortress, but bunkers and barracks survived.
In the interwar years it housed the Polish Radio 'Warszawa II' transmitter (bought from Marconi), with two steel antenna masts about 75 m tall set ~130 m apart.
Heavily bombed by the Germans in September 1939; the station went off air on 23 September and the fort fell on 26 September.
During the 1944 Warsaw Uprising it served as a German command post with a garrison of around 500; today it is occupied by offices.
It stands directly south of Racławicka Street at no. 99.
w: A pentagonal Tsarist fort of the inner Warsaw Fortress ring, ringed by a dry moat -- and, between the wars, the base of Polish Radio's national transmitter, two steel masts 75 metres tall. German bombs silenced the station in September 1939, and the fort fell days later. A 1944 command post; offices fill it now.

hook: A drum-shaped Montalembert artillery tower below the Citadel, on whose slopes Romuald Traugutt and the January Uprising's last leaders were hanged.
facts: Built 1851-1853 as Fort Władimir to shield the Warsaw Citadel from the New Town side and guard a seasonal military bridge over the Vistula.

Designed on the plan of an incomplete brick cylinder after the fortification ideas of Marc-René de Montalembert, with a drawbridge gate, dry ditch and rooftop firing platform; the upper level held positions for 34 cannon.
On 5 August 1864 Romuald Traugutt and fellow January Uprising leaders Żuliński, Toczyski, Krajewski and Jeziorański were executed on its slopes.
Renamed Fort Legionów in 1921; entered in the register of monuments in 1965.
Today it hosts a restaurant and events venue, with guided tours of its underground corridors.
w: A drum-shaped artillery tower below the Citadel, built 1851-53 on Montalembert's plan to guard a Vistula bridge -- its roof once held positions for 34 cannon. On its slopes in 1864 the Russians hanged Romuald Traugutt and the last leaders of the January Uprising. A restaurant fills it now, with tours of the tunnels below.
